1_0: Source: Various Population flows publications and annual reports–Migration Reporting, Department of Home Affairs
1. Excludes Child visas for comparative purposes.
2. From 2015–16 to 2021–22, Child visa outcomes (excluding Orphan Relative visas) were not counted within the Family stream. Orphan Relative visa outcomes, as of 2022–23, are counted under Child.
Child visa outcomes, from 2022–23, count against the Family stream; Orphan Relative visa outcomes, as of 2022–23, are counted under Child.
3. Information from 1984–85 to 1994–95 is rounded and reflects changes in reporting.
Note: Program outcomes do not include New Zealand citizens (before 2017–18) or selected Humanitarian visas such as Secondary Movement Offshore Entry (Temporary),
Secondary Movement Relocation (Temporary) and Temporary Protection visas. Numbers are for net outcome, and provisional visa holders who do not
subsequently obtain permanent visas due to refusal or withdrawal are returned to the Program.

1_1: 1. From 2019–20, due to structural changes, the Employer Sponsored visa category no longer includes regional migration visas.
2. The Regional migration category commenced 1 July 2019.

3.The Global Talent (Independent) program came into effect from 4 November 2019. Effective 6 December 2024, the Global Talent visa (subclass 858) was closed to new applications and was replaced by the National Innovation visa.

4. The Business Innovation and Investment Program closed to new applications on 31 July 2024. Numbers are for net outcome. Visas cancelled under s134 of the Migration Act 1958 are returned to the Program in the year in which they are cancelled.
5. The National Innovation visa commenced 7 December 2024 replacing the Global Talent visa (subclass 858).

6. In effect 1 July 2012, replacing the Family Sponsored and Skilled Independent Regional (Provisional) visas. Effective 1 July 2019, due to structural changes the category ceased and the visa components merged within the new 'Regional' migration category.
7. Includes Partner, Prospective Marriage, Spouse (defunct) and Interdependent (defunct) visa categories.
8. From 2015–16 to 2021–22, Child visa outcomes (excluding Orphan Relative visas) were not counted within the Family stream. Orphan Relative visa outcomes, as of 2022–23, are counted under Child.
9. Includes Aged Dependent Relative, Carer, Special Need Relative, Remaining Relative and, between 2015–16 and 2021–22, Orphan Relative visa categories.
10. From 2022–23, Child visa outcomes count against the Family stream.
Note: From 2023-24, Partner places are allocated on demand as are Child visas (since 2015–16) within the overall Migration Program ceiling. Stream and category places based on 2024–25.

Note: To protect the privacy of individuals, various data confidentiality techniques have been applied. These techniques include:
- data masking — using primary and secondary suppression methods for values that are deemed to be a disclosure risk
- perturbation — a data security technique that allows for random data adjustment to prevent the release of identifiable data.

1_18: 1. The Regional migration category commenced 1 July 2019.

2. The Global Talent (Independent) program came into effect from 4 November 2019. Effective 6 December 2024, the Global Talent visa (subclass 858) was closed to new applications and was replaced by the National Innovation visa.

3. The Business Innovation and Investment Program closed to new applications on 31 July 2024. Numbers are for net outcome. Visas cancelled under s134 of the Migration Act 1958 are returned to the Program in the year in which they are cancelled.

4. In effect 1 July 2012, replacing the Family Sponsored and Skilled Independent Regional (Provisional) visas. Effective 1 July 2019, due to structural changes the category ceased and the visa components merged within the new 'Regional' migration category.
5. From 2015–16 to 2021–22, Child visa outcomes (excluding Orphan Relative visas) were not counted within the Family stream. Orphan Relative visa outcomes, as of 2022–23, are counted under Child.

6. From 2022–23, Child visa outcomes count against the Family stream; from 2023-24, Partner places are allocated on demand as are Child visas (since 2015–16) within the overall Migration Program ceiling.
Note: Includes both primary and secondary applicants. Location is at time of visa lodgement. Stream and category places based on 2024–25.

2_3: 1.Top 15 Visa types are based on 2024–25 visas granted.

2. Effective 1 July 2024, there was a re-alignment of the Temporary Graduate streams, the Post-Study Work stream became the Post-Higher Education Work stream, and the Graduate Work stream became the Post-Vocational Education Work stream.

3. Effective 24 November 2012 as a stream within the Temporary Work (Short Stay Activity) visa (subclass 400) - closed to new applications from 19 November 2016; continued as a stream within the Temporary Work (Short Stay Specialist) visa (subclass 400), which commenced 19 November 2016.
4. Effective 4 April 2022, replacing the Seasonal Worker Program and the Pacific Labour Scheme.
5. A stream within the within the Temporary Activity visa (subclass 408), which came into effect from 19 November 2016.

3_0: Note: Figures were officially revised at the end of 2024–25 and therefore may differ from statistics previously published.

The 2015–16 and 2016–17 statistics include visas granted towards the annual offshore resettlement component of the Humanitarian Program, and the additional 12,000 places for people displaced by conflict in Syria and Iraq.

3_1: 1. Statistics for the offshore component up to 2018–19 are based on country of birth. From 2019–20, statistics are based on a person’s citizenship. The country of birth or citizenship of the principal visa applicant is applied to secondary visa applicants.

3. Data has been perturbed to protect the privacy of individuals.

- perturbation — a data security technique that allows for random data adjustment to prevent the release of identifiable data.
